title: Listening - Part 4: An Artisan Baker
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with Noor Hassan, who owns a small bread bakery. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: My guest today is Noor Hassan, who left an office job to open a small bakery specialising in bread. Noor, were you always interested in baking? || Noor: My family cooked a lot, but bread seemed mysterious to me until I was studying science at college. [24]A teacher used bread-making to explain how yeast works, and I became fascinated by the way a few simple ingredients could change so completely[/24]. I began experimenting at home, then baked for friends while working in an accounts office. || Presenter: Why did you finally decide to open a shop? || Noor: I sold loaves at a weekend market first, and people were encouraging. When the shop opened, though, I made a classic beginner’s mistake. [25]I produced lots of complicated breads because I thought customers wanted novelty, but most people came in looking for a really good everyday loaf[/25]. Once I understood that, the business became much more stable. || Presenter: Bakers are famous for getting up early. How do you cope with that? || Noor: It took time. I certainly don’t jump out of bed happily every morning, especially in January. Yet there’s a special period before the doors open. [26]I enjoy the quiet hours when the bread is baking and the town has not started rushing around yet[/26]. Later, the shop is lively and friendly, but that peaceful beginning helps me concentrate. || Presenter: What do you do with bread you haven’t sold? || Noor: Predicting demand is hard, and I hated putting food in the bin. We reduce prices late in the day, but there is sometimes bread left. [27]A community kitchen now collects our remaining loaves and serves them with evening meals[/27]. It does not solve every waste problem, but knowing the bread is eaten makes a great difference to me and the staff. || Presenter: You recently took on an apprentice. Has that changed your work? || Noor: I thought training somebody would just slow me down. Instead, it has been useful. [28]When I have to explain why I do something, I notice habits that could be improved in my own baking[/28]. My apprentice asks excellent questions, so we both learn. || Presenter: Your photographs online are very popular. Is that helpful? || Noor: Mostly, yes, because people discover the shop. But images can give the wrong message. [29]Online customers sometimes care more about whether a loaf looks perfect in a photograph than whether it has developed a wonderful flavour[/29]. Good bread isn’t always neat and identical. || Presenter: What advice would you give someone planning a bakery? || Noor: Don’t sign an expensive rental agreement just because you love baking at home. [30]Sell at local markets first, because that teaches you what people buy and whether you enjoy producing bread regularly[/30]. A shop is rewarding, but it requires business sense as well as enthusiasm.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What first interested Noor in bread-making?

options.C: Wanting to earn money while at college.

questions.explanation: A teacher demonstrated yeast through bread-making, which fascinated Noor.

questions.question: What did Noor learn soon after opening the bakery?

options.B: Simple high-quality bread was in greatest demand.
options.C: The shop needed a wider selection of cakes.

questions.explanation: Noor produced unusual loaves, but most customers wanted a good everyday loaf.

questions.question: What does Noor like about working early in the morning?

options.A: Being able to finish before midday.
options.C: Having a calm period before the town becomes busy.

questions.explanation: Noor enjoys the quiet hours while bread is baking and the town is not yet busy.

questions.question: How does Noor reduce waste from the bakery?

options.A: Unused bread is collected for community meals.
options.B: Old bread is used to produce new types of loaf.
options.C: Customers are asked to order everything in advance.

questions.explanation: A community kitchen collects remaining loaves and serves them with evening meals.

questions.question: What has Noor discovered through training an apprentice?

options.A: Teaching helps improve Noor's own techniques.
options.B: The apprentice works more quickly than expected.
options.C: Certain recipes are too difficult to teach.

questions.explanation: Explaining methods makes Noor notice personal habits that could be improved.

questions.question: What concern does Noor express about social media?

options.A: Photos encourage customers to judge bread by appearance.
options.B: Online advertising takes too much time to manage.
options.C: People copy recipes without visiting the shop.

questions.explanation: Noor says some online customers care more about perfect-looking loaves than flavour.

questions.question: What does Noor advise future bakery owners to do?

options.A: Study business before learning to bake.
options.B: Begin by selling bread at markets.
options.C: Share premises with another food business.

questions.explanation: Noor recommends testing the work and demand through local market sales first.
